bugs.title: Staged markdown file diffs are duplicated in the agent payload (FR3 violation) and the max_md_lines cap is bypassed
bugs.description: In internal/git/diff.go, the 'other' diff section is captured with the pathspec exclusions ':!.md' and ':!.markdown' (NO wildcard). A git pathspec without a wildcard is a literal/prefix match, so ':!.md' only excludes a file literally named '.md' — it does NOT exclude '*.md'. As a result every staged markdown file's diff is emitted TWICE in the combined payload: once from the per-file markdown capture (FR2) and again from the 'other' section (FR3). The code comment explicitly acknowledges markdown 'intentionally also appears in other_diff', but this directly contradicts PRD FR3 which defines the 'other' section as 'Non-markdown files'. A second, worse consequence: the per-file max_md_lines cap (default 100) is defeated, because the duplicated markdown also flows through the 'other' section which has NO line cap (only the 300,000-byte max_diff_bytes cap). A 500-line markdown doc contributes ~100 capped lines in the md section PLUS all ~500 lines in the other section. Verified empirically: a staged README.md + app.go produced 3 'diff --git' blocks in the captured agent stdin (README.md twice). An in-package test asserting doc.md appears exactly once FAILS. For a tool whose entire value proposition is spending the user's already-paid coding-plan quota efficiently, duplicating diff context is particularly harmful: it wastes quota and degrades commit-message quality.
bugs.reproduction:
1. Build: cd /home/dustin/projects/stagehand-hack && make build
2. Create a repo with one commit, then stage a markdown file and a non-markdown file:
   git init -q /tmp/mdtest && cd /tmp/mdtest && git config user.email t@t.com && git config user.name t && echo init>a.go && git add a.go && git commit -q -m init
   printf '## Hello\n' > README.md && echo code >> a.go && git add README.md a.go
3. Configure a stub provider that captures stdin:
   cat > .stagehand.toml <<'EOF'
   [provider.stub]
   detect = "stubagent"
   command = "stubagent"
   prompt_delivery = "stdin"
   output = "raw"
   strip_code_fence = true
   EOF
   (build the stubagent at internal/generate/testdata/stubagent and put it on PATH)
4. STAGEHAND_STUB_STDIN=/tmp/cap.txt STAGEHAND_STUB_SCRIPT='[{"emit":"x"}]' ./bin/stagehand --provider stub --dry-run
5. Inspect /tmp/cap.txt: grep -c '^diff --git a/README.md' /tmp/cap.txt returns 2 (expected 1).
bugs.expected: Per PRD FR3, the 'other' diff section contains ONLY non-markdown files (markdown is captured separately per FR2 and concatenated per FR4). Each staged markdown file's diff appears exactly once in the combined payload, and max_md_lines caps every markdown file's contribution to 100 lines.
bugs.actual: Each staged .md/.markdown file (including nested paths like docs/readme.md) appears twice, and large markdown files leak their full content (uncapped by max_md_lines) through the 'other' section. Verified: 3 diff blocks instead of 2; a 500-line big.md produced ~594 'line N' entries in the payload instead of ~100.
bugs.location: internal/git/diff.go, StagedDiff(), the g.run("diff", "--cached", "--", ...) call whose args include ":!.md" and ":!.markdown".
bugs.suggestedFix: Change the two no-wildcard pathspec tokens to the wildcarded form that actually matches markdown files across all directories: replace ":!.md" with ":!*.md" and ":!.markdown" with ":!*.markdown". Verified via direct `git diff --cached -- ... ':!*.md' ':!*.markdown'` that this excludes nested *.md and *.markdown correctly, leaving only the non-markdown files. Also add a regression test asserting each staged markdown file appears exactly once in StagedDiff's output (the existing TestStagedDiff_MdBeforeOther only checks HasPrefix + Contains, which is why this slipped through).

bugs.title: FR29 default retry_instruction is never applied for 5 of 6 built-in providers (claude, gemini, opencode, codex, cursor)
bugs.description: PRD FR29 states: 'On empty/failed parse, retry generation once with a corrective instruction (per the manifest's retry_instruction, default: "Output only the commit message, with no preamble, no markdown, and no quotes.")'. The generate inner loop (internal/generate/generate.go) only swaps the instruction slot when `deps.Manifest.RetryInstruction != ""`. Only the pi built-in manifest sets RetryInstruction; claude, gemini, opencode, codex, and cursor all leave it empty. For those five providers, the FR29-mandated DEFAULT corrective instruction is never substituted, so the parse-correction retry rebuilds the payload with the identical original instruction ('Generate a commit message for these changes:') — a plain re-roll with no corrective guidance. Verified empirically: a stub provider with no retry_instruction emitting empty output on call 0 re-sent the original instruction on the retry, whereas the same provider with retry_instruction set sent 'Output ONLY the commit message. No preamble, no markdown, no quotes.' as specified.
bugs.reproduction:
1. Configure two stub providers, identical except one omits retry_instruction:
   [provider.noretry] command="stubagent" prompt_delivery="stdin" output="raw" strip_code_fence=true
   [provider.withretry] ... retry_instruction="Output ONLY the commit message. No preamble, no markdown, no quotes."
2. Stage a change; run with a stub script that emits EMPTY on call 0 and a valid message on call 1, capturing stdin (STAGEHAND_STUB_STDIN) and using STAGEHAND_STUB_STATE to advance the cross-process counter.
3. `stagehand --provider noretry -v` then inspect the captured retry stdin: the trailing instruction is 'Generate a commit message for these changes:'.
4. `stagehand --provider withretry -v`: the retry stdin ends with 'Output ONLY the commit message. No preamble, no markdown, no quotes.'.
bugs.expected: When a built-in provider's manifest does not set retry_instruction, the FR29 default ('Output only the commit message, with no preamble, no markdown, and no quotes.') is applied on the parse-correction retry for every provider.
bugs.actual: Only pi sends a corrective instruction on retry. The other five built-in providers re-send the original instruction, so the corrective retry provides no correction (deterministic agents would fail identically; non-deterministic ones only benefit from a blind re-roll).
bugs.location: internal/generate/generate.go (the `if deps.Manifest.RetryInstruction != "" { instr = deps.Manifest.RetryInstruction }` block in the inner loop) combined with internal/provider/builtin.go (claude/gemini/opencode/codex/cursor omit RetryInstruction).
bugs.suggestedFix: Either (a) apply the FR29 default string inside the generate loop when Manifest.RetryInstruction == "" (treat empty as the documented default), or (b) bake the default into every built-in manifest / the registry merge so all six providers carry a RetryInstruction. Option (a) is the smaller change and matches the FR29 wording ('default: ...').

bugs.title: Public library API (pkg/stagehand.GenerateCommit) installs a process-killing SIGINT/SIGTERM handler, breaking documented library embedding (US12)
bugs.description: pkg/stagehand.GenerateCommit is documented 'Stable as of v1.0' and US12 explicitly targets embedding commit-message generation 'in a larger tool'. However, GenerateCommit delegates to internal/generate.CommitStaged, which unconditionally calls installSignalHandler after git write-tree. That handler's goroutine calls `exit(handleSignal(...))` where `exit` is the package-level `var exit = os.Exit` (unexported, so a library caller cannot override it). Consequence: if an integrator embeds GenerateCommit and the user presses Ctrl-C (or the host receives SIGTERM) during the generation window, the ENTIRE host process is terminated with exit code 3 — not just the generation cancelled. A library must never install a process-global signal handler that calls os.Exit. A compounding issue: restoreSignalHandler is only invoked on the dry-run path and the success path (immediately before update-ref); it is NOT called on ANY error/rescue path (run error, parse-fail, dup-exhaustion, CommitTree error, git-query errors). So after GenerateCommit returns a rescue error, the handler remains armed with the STALE treeSHA closure — a later SIGINT would render a rescue block for the old snapshot and os.Exit(3). (Note: this behavior is mandated by PRD §18.4 for the CLI default action, so the fix should preserve CLI behavior while making the library path safe.)
bugs.reproduction: Code-path verification (no external agent needed): `grep -n 'installSignalHandler\|restoreSignalHandler' internal/generate/generate.go` shows install at the post-snapshot point and restore only at the dry-run and success sites. `grep -n 'var exit\|exit(handleSignal' internal/generate/signal.go` shows the goroutine calls os.Exit. To demonstrate dynamically: write a small Go program that calls stagehand.GenerateCommit(ctx, opts) against a stub agent that hangs, send SIGINT to the program, and observe the whole program exits 3 instead of GenerateCommit returning a context.Canceled error.
bugs.expected: An integrator using the public, v1-stable GenerateCommit API can cancel an in-flight generation (or have one interrupted by a signal) without the host process being killed, and can rely on the function returning an error rather than the process terminating. Per US12, embedding should be safe.
bugs.actual: Any SIGINT/SIGTERM arriving during generation triggers os.Exit(3) for the whole process; there is no public opt-out, and the `exit` seam is unexported. Error paths additionally leak an armed, stale handler.
bugs.location: internal/generate/signal.go (installSignalHandler goroutine + var exit = os.Exit) and internal/generate/generate.go (unconditional installSignalHandler; restoreSignalHandler absent from error paths).
bugs.suggestedFix: Make signal-handler installation injectable/optional for the library surface: e.g., add a Deps/Options flag (InstallSignalHandler bool, default true for the CLI, false when called via pkg/stagehand) or pass a no-op signal installer from GenerateCommit. Additionally call restoreSignalHandler (and signal.Reset) on every return path after install, including all rescue/error returns, so no stale handler ever outlives CommitStaged. Preserve the CLI's existing exit-3-on-Ctrl-C behavior by keeping the handler installed for the cmd/stagehand path.

bugs.title: `stagehand --help` lists every global flag twice
bugs.description: The root command's Long text (cmd/stagehand/main.go rootLong) embeds a hand-written 'Global flags (...)' block listing all nine flags, and cobra additionally auto-generates its own 'Flags:' block from the registered persistent flags. The result is that every flag (e.g. --provider) appears twice in `stagehand --help` output, which is redundant and looks unfinished. Verified: `stagehand --help | grep -c -- '--provider'` returns 2.
bugs.reproduction: Run `./bin/stagehand --help` and observe two separate listings of the same flags (the manual block under 'Global flags' and cobra's auto-generated 'Flags' block). `./bin/stagehand --help | grep -cE '^\s+--provider'` returns 2.
bugs.expected: Each flag appears exactly once in the help output.
bugs.actual: Each flag appears twice (manual block + cobra auto block).
bugs.location: cmd/stagehand/main.go — the rootLong constant includes a manual flag listing while registerPersistentFlags also registers them as persistent flags (cobra renders them automatically).
bugs.suggestedFix: Remove the manual 'Global flags' block from rootLong (keep only the synopsis + usage + a pointer to docs), letting cobra render the single canonical flag list. Alternatively set a custom usage template. Either way, ensure one listing.

bugs.title: HEAD-moved diagnostic omits the PARENT/actual SHAs that PRD §13.5 specifies
bugs.description: PRD §13.5 specifies the HEAD-moved message should read: 'HEAD moved from <PARENT> to <actual> while generating; aborting to avoid a non-fast-forward. ...'. The implemented message (internal/generate/generate.go, UpdateRefCAS failure branch) prints only 'HEAD moved while generating; aborting to avoid a non-fast-forward.' — it omits both the expected parent SHA and the actual current HEAD SHA, so the user cannot see what HEAD moved to. The printed manual recovery command still contains the parent SHA and tree, so the path remains actionable; this is a diagnostic-wording deviation from the spec, not a data-integrity issue. Verified empirically by simulating a concurrent commit during generation (exit 1, message lacked the SHAs).
bugs.reproduction: Use a provider wrapper that runs `git commit --allow-empty -m concurrent` to move HEAD during generation, then run stagehand. The output begins 'HEAD moved while generating; aborting to avoid a non-fast-forward.' with no SHA values, followed by the recovery command.
bugs.expected: The diagnostic names both the expected parent SHA and the actual current HEAD value, per PRD §13.5: 'HEAD moved from <PARENT> to <actual> while generating...'.
bugs.actual: The diagnostic says only 'HEAD moved while generating' with no SHAs (the underlying UpdateRefCAS error already carries the actual ref value, but it is not surfaced).
bugs.location: internal/generate/generate.go — the UpdateRefCAS error-handling branch (Progressf 'HEAD moved while generating; aborting to avoid a non-fast-forward.').
bugs.suggestedFix: Surface the expected (parentSHA) and actual (the value git reports, parseable from the typed *git.ExitError stderr / a separate `git rev-parse HEAD`) SHAs in the message, matching PRD §13.5. The actual HEAD value is already available from the failed update-ref's stderr (' ... cannot lock ref ... is at <sha> but expected <sha>') or from a fresh RevParseHEAD after the failure.

summary: End-to-end validation of the v1 stagehand implementation against the PRD, performed with an isolated config (XDG_CONFIG_HOME) and a stub agent driving the real binary. The snapshot-based atomic-commit core is solid: stage-while-generating correctly isolates snapshot-time files, the CAS HEAD-moved path refuses to force-update and prints recovery, the rescue protocol fires on timeout/parse-failure/duplicate-exhaustion with correct exit codes (124 for timeout, 3 for rescue, 2 for nothing-to-commit), duplicate-rejection retries work, root commits are handled, and dry-run keeps stdout byte-clean. The build is green (all packages pass). However, five issues were found. The most impactful is BUG-001: every staged markdown file's diff is duplicated in the agent payload because the ':!.md' pathspec lacks a wildcard and does not exclude markdown from the 'other' diff section (FR3 violation); this also bypasses the max_md_lines cap and wastes the user's coding-plan quota — the very thing the tool exists to protect. BUG-002 means the FR29 corrective retry instruction is a no-op for 5 of 6 built-in providers. BUG-003 makes the documented stable library API unsafe to embed because it installs an os.Exit-calling signal handler. BUG-004 and BUG-005 are cosmetic/diagnostic polish. None of the issues corrupt the repository (the §18.1 invariant holds in every path tested), but BUG-001 in particular should be fixed before release because it systematically degrades the primary value proposition.
recommendations: Fix BUG-001 first: it is the highest-impact issue (quota waste + message-quality regression + FR3/FR2 non-compliance) and is a one-token pathspec change (':!.md' -> ':!*.md', ':!.markdown' -> ':!*.markdown') plus a regression test asserting each markdown file appears exactly once in StagedDiff output.
Fix BUG-002 by applying the FR29 default retry_instruction when the manifest field is empty, so all six providers send corrective guidance on a parse-retry.

Reconcile BUG-003 with PRD §18.4: keep the CLI's exit-3-on-Ctrl-C behavior, but make signal-handler installation opt-out for the pkg/stagehand library surface and restore/disarm the handler on every return path (including errors) so embedding is safe per US12.
Address BUG-004 (remove the duplicate flag block in --help) and BUG-005 (include PARENT/actual SHAs in the HEAD-moved message) as release-polish.

Strengthen the test suite with negative assertions: TestStagedDiff_MdBeforeOther should assert markdown appears exactly once (not just HasPrefix/Contains), and add a provider-agnostic test that the retry payload carries a corrective instruction for every built-in provider.
